coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots modified molybdenum disulfide as a high-efficiency hydrogen evolution electrocatalyst
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: You, Jia
Jia, Zhijun
Wang, Yi
Wang, Di
Song, Jing
Tian, Liangliang
Qi, Tao
coredata.dc:description: Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) is considered a low-cost material that may replace platinum-based electrocatalysts towards hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). However, the catalytic activity of MoS2 is limited by the low conductivity and lack of active sites. Here, a biomass carbon dots-molybdenum disulfide (BCDs-MoS2) composite was synthesized as a HER catalyst by a simple hydrothermal method. The BCDs-MoS2 catalyst displayed excellent electrocatalytic performance for HER with lower onset overpotential (115 mV), smaller Tafel slope (56.57 mV dec−1) as well as high cycling stability, which superior to those of homemade MoS2, commercial MoS2, and most of the reported MoS2-based catalysts. According to the characterization results of morphology, surface properties, and valence states of elements, the outstanding catalytic activity of BCDs-MoS2 is ascribed to its loose structure with a large specific surface area along with abundant edges and defects, and the increase in the amount of S2 2− and S2− which possess higher activity due to the addition of the BCDs. This study can afford a new strategy to design high performance HER catalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molybdenum disulfide
Biomass carbon dots
Hydrogen evolution reaction
Electrocatalysts
Electron interaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of commercial MoS2, homemade MoS2, BCDs-MoS2, and BCDs. SEM images of (b) commercial MoS2, (c) homemade MoS2, and (d) BCDs-MoS2. (e) EDS elemental mapping images of BCDs-MoS2.
(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of BCDs-MoS2. (c) TEM and (d) HRTEM images of BCDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of BCDs-MoS2. High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) Mo 3d and (c) S 2p for BCDs-MoS2, homemade MoS2, and commercial MoS2.
N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of (a) homemade MoS2 and (b) BCDs-MoS2.
(a) LSV curves of GCE, commercial MoS2, homemade MoS2, BCDs, and BCDs-MoS2. (b) Tafel plots of commercial MoS2, homemade MoS2, and BCDs-MoS2.
(a) EIS of commercial MoS2, homemade MoS2, and BCDs-MoS2. (b) Partial enlargement view of (a).
CV curves of (a) homemade MoS2, (b) BCDs-MoS2 in 0.5 M H2SO4 at different scan rates. (c) Measured capacitive currents plotted as a function of scan rate.

(a) LSV curves before and after 1000 cycles of CVs for BCDs-MoS2, (b) chronoamperometry curve of BCDs-MoS2, (c) SEM image of BCDs-MoS2 after 1000 cycles of CVs, (d) XRD patterns of BCDs-MoS2 before and after 1000 cycles of CVs (the scan rate of CVs is 50 mV s−1).
Schematic illustration for the synthesis of BCDs-MoS2.
Binding energy (B.E.) and relative intensity of XPS spectra of Mo species in the BCDs-MoS2, homemade MoS2 and commercial MoS2 catalysts.
Binding energy (B.E.) and relative intensity of XPS spectra of S species in the BCDs-MoS2, homemade MoS2 and commercial MoS2 catalysts.
Comparison of onset overpotential and Tafel slope of homemade MoS2, BCDs-MoS2 with those of other MoS2-based electrocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots modified molybdenum disulfide as a high-efficiency hydrogen evolution electrocatalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Material Science and Engineering College, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Material Science and Engineering College, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Research Center of Green Recycling for Strategic Metal Resources, Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center of Green Recycling for Strategic Metal Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Engineering Research Center of Green Recycling for Strategic Metal Resources, Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronic Information and Electrical Engineering, Chongqing University of Arts and Sciences, Chongqing 400000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electronic Information and Electrical Engineering
Chongqing University of Arts and Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electronic Information and Electrical Engineering, Chongqing University of Arts and Sciences, Chongqing 400000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) is considered a low-cost material that may replace platinum-based electrocatalysts towards hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). However, the catalytic activity of MoS2 is limited by the low conductivity and lack of active sites. Here, a biomass carbon dots-molybdenum disulfide (BCDs-MoS2) composite was synthesized as a HER catalyst by a simple hydrothermal method. The BCDs-MoS2 catalyst displayed excellent electrocatalytic performance for HER with lower onset overpotential (115 mV), smaller Tafel slope (56.57 mV dec−1) as well as high cycling stability, which superior to those of homemade MoS2, commercial MoS2, and most of the reported MoS2-based catalysts. According to the characterization results of morphology, surface properties, and valence states of elements, the outstanding catalytic activity of BCDs-MoS2 is ascribed to its loose structure with a large specific surface area along with abundant edges and defects, and the increase in the amount of S2 2− and S2− which possess higher activity due to the addition of the BCDs. This study can afford a new strategy to design high performance HER catalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: The world is faced with a threat of energy crises, such as fossil energy shortages and traditional fuel pollution [1]. The search for sustainable clean energy is an urgent task. At present, the widely studied clean energy includes wind energy, solar energy, biological energy, ocean energy, geothermal energy, and hydrogen energy, among which hydrogen energy is considered an energy source with great potential for development and a wide range of applications [2,3]. Hydrogen fuel possesses high-weight chemical energy up to 142 MJ kg−1 (3 times higher than that of fossil fuels or other chemical fuels) [4] and is an ideal substitute for fossil fuels [1,5–7]. Among various hydrogen production approaches, electrochemical water splitting is believed to be a safe and efficient method to obtain high-purity hydrogen [8]. However, hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) on cathode and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) on anode need a large overpotential during the water electrolysis process, which thus impedes the large-scale preparation of hydrogen [9]. Therefore, developing highly active and stable catalysts for the HER and OER is highly desirable. It is well known that noble metal catalysts, especially platinum, have been proved to be the state-of-the-art electrocatalyst towards HER due to the low overpotential and enhanced electrocatalytic stability. Nevertheless, they are of high cost and scarce, which greatly limits their extensive applications in commercialization [10,11]. In consequence, various non-Pt-based electrocatalysts with an affordable price and abundant raw materials have been extensively studied for electrocatalytic water splitting [12–15].

Recently, transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs), as a type of nonprecious electrocatalysts for HER in acidic media, have attracted widespread attention owing to their highly catalytic efficiency and stability [16,17]. As one of TMDs, MoS2 is a stable, nontoxic, and low-cost material, which has shown great potential in the field of electrocatalysis for HER [18,19]. However, although MoS2 has been investigated as the most promising alternative to Pt-based HER electrocatalyst, its low conductivity and limited exposure of active sites hinder its practical application [20]. Various approaches have been proposed to improve the activity of MoS2, such as exposing more active sites, compositing with high-conductivity materials and doping with metal atoms to improve the intrinsic activity of edges [21–23].

Owing to the high stability, large specific surface area, and excellent conductivity, carbon nanomaterials, including one-dimensional carbon nanofibers [24,25] and carbon nanotubes [26] and two-dimensional graphene [27,28], have been used to combine with MoS2 for high electro-catalysis efficiency [29]. It is indicated that carbon nanomaterials can not only increase the exposed active sites and conductivity of MoS2, but also suppress its aggregation and stacking during durability tests [30]. Zero-dimensional carbon dots (CDs), as a novel type of carbon nanomaterials, have attracted more interest because of their strong fluorescence, fast electron transfer speed, and strong electron storage capability [31]. Yet, to the best of our knowledge, there is no report about MoS2-based catalysts combined with CDs for the HER. Furthermore, as compared with CDs prepared from chemical raw materials, biomass carbon dots (BCDs) made from natural materials which possess complex components can generate various surface groups and present special performance [32]. In addition, natural raw materials are usually environmentally friendly and abundant. This aroused our interest in the effects of BCDs on the MoS2 catalyst. In this work, a kind of BCDs was synthesized using chitosan as raw material, which was extracted from waste shrimp shells and crab shells. Then a BCDs-MoS2 composite material was prepared by a simple hydrothermal method, and it was found that this material exhibited excellent electrocatalytic performance towards HER, which even exceeds the performance of most of HER electrocatalysts.

The BCDs were prepared using a simple hydrothermal method. Citric acid, chitosan, and deionized water were added to the beaker and stirred evenly at room temperature for 1 h. Then the mixture was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave and hydrothermally treated at 160 °C for 5 h. The suspension was subsequently cooled to room temperature naturally. Then the suspension was poured out, and ultrafiltration was performed to obtain the BCDs solution.

One-step hydrothermal synthesis of BCDs-MoS2 is shown in Scheme 1 . Thioacetamide (CH3CSNH2), sodium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O), and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) were dissolved in the BCDs solution. After stirring evenly for 30 min, the suspension was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave, hydrothermally treated at 180 °C for 30 h, and then naturally cooled to room temperature. Afterwards, the reaction product was washed 3 times with deionized water and absolute ethanol. Finally, the as-synthesized sample was dried at 60 °C. Homemade MoS2 was prepared by the same route, except that the BCDs solution was replaced by deionized water.

An X-ray diffractometer (XRD, X'Pert PRO MPD) was used to analyze the composition and structure of the catalysts. Content of BCDs in the BCDs-MoS2 composite was measured by C/N elements analyzer (CN802, VELP). Thermal field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7610F), energy dispersive X-ray energy spectroscopy (EDS, JSM-7610F), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-F200 (cyro)), and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM2100F) were used to analyze the morphology and element dispersion status of the materials. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi) was used to analyze the chemical state of the sample surface. Conductivities of materials were obtained by four-probe powder conductivity measuring instrument (ST2722). Nitrogen sorption measurements (Micromeritics ASAP2020 Analyzer) were performed for the calculation of specific surface area of catalysts and pore size distribution using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) methods, respectively.

The electrochemical measurements were conducted by a three-electrode cell using an electrochemical workstation (CHI760D, Chenhua, Shanghai), and the electrolyte was 0.5 M H2SO4 saturated with N2. A platinum foil was used as the counter electrode, and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was used as the reference electrode. The electrocatalyst ink was prepared by dispersing 10 mg of catalysts in 2 mL of ethanol by ultrasonic treatment. The working electrode was prepared by dropping 5 μL of electrocatalyst ink onto glassy carbon electrode (GCE) and using 2 μL of 0.5% Nafion to fix catalysts after the ink was dried. All potentials were calibrated to the reversible hydrogen electrode: E (vs RHE) = E (vs SCE) + 0.0592 pH + 0.242 V = E (vs SCE) + 0.242 V (pH = 0).

Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves and Tafel plots were measured in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The potentials obtained from LSV experiments were corrected for iR drop. The cyclic voltammograms (CVs) were measured from −0.15 to −0.03 V at different scan rates (from 20 to 180 mV s−1, interval is 20 mV s−1) for obtaining the electrical double-layer capacitances (Cdl). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed at a frequency range from 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz with an AC perturbation amplitude of 5 mV in 0.5 M H2SO4. Stability test was carried out by CV scanning from 0 to −0.6 V for 1000 laps at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1.

XRD measurement was performed to detect the crystal structure of the prepared catalysts. In Fig. 1 (a), the crystallinity of BCDs-MoS2 is significantly lower than that of commercial MoS2 and slightly lower than that of homemade MoS2, and the corresponding XRD pattern of BCDs shows a broad peak at a center position of approximately 20° because the carbon dots are highly amorphous [33]. Moreover, it is difficult to observe the characteristic peaks of carbon diffraction in the pattern of BCDs-MoS2, which may be due to the lower content (the mass percentage of C in the BCDs-MoS2 composite was measured to be 3.979% by C/N elements analyzer) and amorphous state of the BCDs. Both the homemade MoS2 and BCDs-MoS2 samples present the diffraction peaks at approximately 14.0°, 28.3°, 32.3°, 35.9°, 43.4° and 57.4°, which are assigned to the (002), (004), (100), (102), (006), and (110) facets of MoS2, respectively, suggesting the formation of MoS2 in these two samples. It was reported that crystalline MoS2 shows worse catalytic performance than amorphous MoS2 [34]. This is because the Mo–Mo bond is much shorter in amorphous MoS2, which is a key factor in the enhancement of its catalytic performance [35]. In the meantime, compared with crystalline MoS2, amorphous MoS2 exhibits a disordered atomic structure, which adds more intrinsic catalytic sites, such as defects and edge-like sites [36]. Therefore, the lower crystallinity of the prepared MoS2 may be beneficial to the improvement of its catalytic performance for HER. The SEM images of the commercial MoS2, homemade MoS2, and BCDs-MoS2 are displayed in Fig. 1(b–d), respectively. The commercial MoS2 is composed of many large sheets stacked together, and there are few edge sites, as shown in Fig. 1(b). Fig. 1(c) reveals that the homemade MoS2 is spherical, and the diameter of the particle is about 800 nm. However, Fig. 1(d) shows that the morphology of the BCDs-MoS2 composite is significantly different from the former two. Specifically, this composite material presents a loose structure composed of many small nanosheets (the side length is approximately 100 nm). More MoS2 edges are obviously exposed in BCDs-MoS2 compared to the homemade and commercial MoS2, which provides more S sites for HER, and thus may be conducive to the improvement of electrocatalytic activity of MoS2 [37]. Additionally, as displayed in Fig. 1(e), Mo and S have a uniform distribution together with C, N, and O, indicating the uniform mixing of MoS2 nanosheets and BCDs, as well as the abundant existence of oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the BCDs.

TEM images show the microstructures of BCDs-MoS2 and BCDs (Fig. 2 ). In Fig. 2(a), the image further confirms the typical stacking nanosheets structure of BCDs-MoS2. Moreover, the dimension of the nanosheets is about 100 nm, which is consistent with Fig. 1(d). In the HRTEM image of BCDs-MoS2 (Fig. 2(b)), it is seen that the lattice fringes with 0.62 nm interlayer spacing are attributed to the (002) lattice plane of MoS2, and the interlayer spacing of BCDs is measured to be 0.21 nm. In addition, some lattice defects can also be observed, for example, cracks shown in the red circles. These arisen defects could act as new catalytic sites for HER [38,39]. Fig. 2(c) and (d) presents that the size of BCDs is approximately 5 nm, and they are spherical in shape, which corresponds with the size requirement for carbon dots [40,].

Fig. 3 displays the XPS spectra, which are used to analyze the surface element valence states and electron interaction in catalysts. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the BCDs-MoS2 composite consists of C, O, N, S, and Mo and is without other impurities, which is consistent with the EDS results in Fig. 1(e). The high-resolution Mo 3d XPS spectra for BCDs-MoS2, homemade MoS2, and commercial MoS2 are shown in Fig. 3(b). It is worth noting that the Mo 3d XPS spectra of BCDs-MoS2 and homemade MoS2 exhibit significant negative shifts compared to that of commercial MoS2, suggesting that more lower valence Mo exists in these two catalysts. This remarkable difference may be due to the different synthesis methods of these two catalysts and commercial MoS2, which leads to their very different morphology and thus different surface state [42]. As for the BCDs-MoS2 and homemade MoS2, it seems that there is no significant difference between their spectra. Table 1 lists the binding energies and relative intensity of Mo4+, MoƟ+ and Mo6+ of these three electrocatalysts evaluated by XPS analysis software Thermo Avantage (MoƟ+ may be due to the incomplete decomposition of MoO4 2− [43,44]). It can be found that the relative intensity of Mo4+ in BCDs-MoS2 is higher as compared with homemade MoS2, indicating that more lower valence Mo exists in the former one. Plus, it has been reported that the lower valence Mo is responsible for higher catalytic activity [45]. This suggests that BCDs-MoS2 may possess preferable catalytic activity. Additionally, the typical XPS spectra for S 2p of these three catalysts were displayed in Fig. 3(c). According to the work of Benck, the peak centered at 163.7 eV manifested the existence of bridging S2 2− and/or apical S2− ligand [46]. As for the high-energy peak at 169.4 eV, it might be a characteristic of the S4+ species in SO3 2−. These groups may locate at the edges of MoS2 layers, which were still present even after washing [43,47]. Table 2 lists the binding energies and relative intensity of S2−, S2 2−/S2−, and S4+ of these three catalysts evaluated by XPS analysis software Thermo Avantage. It can be found from Table 2 that the relative intensity of S4+ (4.0%) of BCDs-MoS2 is substantially lower as compared to that of the homemade MoS2 (10.2%) and commercial MoS2 (16.3%). It was reported that BCDs are excellent electron donor/acceptor, and thus there may be a strong electronic interaction between the BCDs and MoS2 [48–50]. From the XPS spectra and the EDS mapping images (Fig. 1(e)), the BCDs contain abundant nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing functional groups, which are known to be strong electron-donating groups. Therefore, some electrons may be allowed to reduce the S4+ via the nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing functional groups on the BCDs, which leads to a decrease in the amount of S4+ and accordingly an increase in the amount of S2 2− and S2− in the BCDs-MoS2 composite. More S2 2− and S2− might provide more highly active edge sites for HER, which promotes the adsorption of H+ [51–53].

Fig. 4 presents the nitrogen adsorption and desorption curves of homemade MoS2 and BCDs-MoS2 at 77 K. According to the isotherms, the calculated specific surface areas of homemade MoS2 and BCDs-MoS2 are 13.3781 and 122.6449 m2 g−1, respectively. Our previous work has demonstrated that the specific surface area of commercial MoS2 is 1.53 m2 g−1 [42]. It is clear that the specific surface area of homemade MoS2 is one order of magnitude larger than that of commercial MoS2, and the specific surface area of BCDs-MoS2 is one order of magnitude larger than that of homemade MoS2. Evidently, this is related to their completely different morphology in the light of the SEM images shown in Fig. 1. Moreover, as compared with homemade MoS2 and commercial MoS2, the isotherm of BCDs-MoS2 has a more obvious hysteresis loop, depicting that it has a typical mesoporous structure. This suggests that the addition of BCDs causes MoS2 to form more abundant mesoporous. The increase in the specific surface area and the formation of the typical mesoporous structure can provide more active areas and at the same time promote the diffusion of H+.

In 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, the electrocatalytic activity of GCE, BCDs, BCDs-MoS2, homemade MoS2, and commercial MoS2 for HER was tested by the LSV, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 (a). It is found that GCE and BCDs hardly exhibit cathode current within the test potential range, illustrating that GCE and BCDs have little catalytic activity for HER. However, compared with homemade MoS2 and commercial MoS2, BCDs-MoS2 has much higher catalytic activity. When the cathode current density is 10 mA cm−2, the overpotential of BCDs-MoS2 is approximately 232 mV, and the onset overpotential of 115 mV is much lower than that of homemade MoS2 (236.5 mV) and commercial MoS2 (281.5 mV). The Tafel slopes of commercial MoS2, homemade MoS2, and BCDs-MoS2 are calculated to elucidate the underlying mechanism (Fig. 5(b)), and the corresponding values are 162.03, 84.84, and 56.57 mV dec−1, respectively. The results demonstrate that BCDs-MoS2 has faster reaction kinetics, and the Volmer-Heyrovsky mechanism indicates the rate-determining step [54,55]. Besides, the catalytic activity of BCDs-MoS2 and homemade MoS2 is compared with that of other reported advanced MoS2-based HER electrocatalysts in Table 3 . It is found that the onset overpotential of BCDs-MoS2 is lower than that of most reported materials. Specifically, except MoS2/Co9S8/CC (73 mV) and N-doped MoS2/Ti3C2TX (80 mV), the onset overpotential (115 mV) of BCDs-MoS2 is the lowest among these MoS2-based electrocatalysts. Furthermore, the Tafel slope of BCDs-MoS2 is lower than those of all MoS2-based catalysts listed in this table. Obviously, the BCDs-MoS2 catalyst possesses relatively high catalytic activity among MoS2-based electrocatalysts towards HER.

The EIS were tested for taking a close look at internal electrochemical properties of catalysts. The charge transfer resistances (Rct) of the three electrocatalysts are obtained by simulation of equivalent circuits in Fig. 6 (a). Fig. 6(b) is a partial enlargement view of Fig. 6(a), which shows more clearly the comparison of EIS of homemade MoS2 and BCDs-MoS2. It is obvious that the Rct decreases from 7015 Ω (commercial MoS2) and 344 Ω (homemade MoS2) to 70.5 Ω (BCDs-MoS2), which implies that the BCDs-MoS2 has a faster electron transfer between the catalyst and the electrolyte.

The number of active sites on catalyst was evaluated through Cdl. The CVs at different scan rates and the linear relationships between current density and scan rate are shown in Fig. 7 . According to the fitting results, the Cdl values of homemade MoS2 and BCDs-MoS2 are 11.55 and 23.29 mF cm−2, respectively. The Cdl of BCDs-MoS2 is over twice as high as that of homemade MoS2, which means that there are more active sites in BCDs-MoS2. This may be ascribed to the fact that the BCDs separated the initially agglomerated flake-like MoS2, delivering more catalytically active sites. A larger specific surface area and loose structure with lattice defects confirmed by SEM, TEM and N2 adsorption isotherms also suggest the role of the BCDs in BCDs-MoS2.

Based on the above analysis, it is obvious that the effect of the BCDs on improving the catalytic activity of MoS2 for HER can be summarized into two aspects. Firstly, the addition of the BCDs greatly changed the morphology of MoS2. A loose structure consisting of MoS2 nanosheets with many defects formed, which possesses a much larger specific surface area and more edges. This structure can provide more catalytically active sites for HER. Secondly, according to XPS analysis, due to the electron-donating property of nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing functional groups on the BCDs, the amount of S4+ decreased and accordingly the amount of S2 2− and S2− increased in the BCDs-MoS2 catalyst, which results in higher activity of sulfur-containing edge sites. Additionally, the conductivities of the catalysts were also measured to further explore the effect of the BCDs. It is obtained that the conductivity of BCDs-MoS2 (7.46 × 10−5 S m−1) is much higher than that of commercial MoS2 (3.58 × 10−8 S m−1). However, it is interesting to find that the conductivity of the former is slightly lower than that of homemade MoS2 (1.67 × 10−4 S m−1), which may be ascribed to the loose structure of the BCDs-MoS2. This also indicates that although conductivity might play a vital role, it is not the only parameter that decides the catalytic activity. The additional active sites in BCDs-MoS2 derived from its mesoporous structure, amorphous nature, and unique morphological effects may have played a predominant role in the higher HER activity.

Besides catalytic activity, stability is another important index of catalyst. To test the catalytic durability, the LSV curves of BCDs-MoS2 before and after 1000 CV cycles are compared in Fig. 8 (a). It is seen that two LSV curves nearly overlap with each other except a tiny difference in current density, showing that the BCDs-MoS2 catalyst not only has excellent catalytic activity, but also possesses favorable long-term stability in electrochemical process. Additionally, the chrono-amperometry curves observed in Fig. 8(b) indicate that the current density on BCDs-MoS2 catalyst is always significantly higher than that on homemade MoS2 catalyst in the whole-time range of 20 h. Besides, the microscopic morphology and structure of BCDs-MoS2 after 1000 cycles of CVs were characterized by SEM and XRD, which are shown in Fig. 8(c) and (d), respectively. It is seen that the characteristic peaks of BCDs-MoS2 are well preserved, and the morphology has little change in contrast to the BCDs-MoS2 before the CV cycles.

In this work, BCDs were prepared using chitosan and citric acid, and the compounding process of MoS2 with BCDs was conducted by a simple hydrothermal method. Then the electrocatalytic activity of the BCDs-MoS2 composite for HER was investigated and compared with that of BCDs, homemade MoS2, and commercial MoS2. It was found that the BCDs-MoS2 composite showed a significantly higher catalytic activity than those catalysts and most of the reported MoS2-based catalysts. According to the characterization results of morphology, surface properties, and valence states of elements, it is clear that the addition of BCDs plays a vital role in improving the electrocatalytic performance of MoS2 catalysts. On the one hand, the MoS2 generates larger specific surface area and more edges owing to BCDs, and the acquiring mesoporous structure and unique morphological effects provide more catalytically active sites for HER. On the other hand, the S4+ is reduced to S2 2− and S2− by nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing functional groups of BCDs, leading to higher activity of sulfur-containing edge sites in the BCDs-MoS2 catalyst. In addition, the BCDs-MoS2 catalyst also exhibited favorable long-term stability besides the excellent catalytic activity.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2020YFC1909001), the “Strategic Priority Research Program” of Chinese Academy of Sciences (Grant No. XDA21070302), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51804291), and the Beijing Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 2184127), as well as the cooperation project between universities in Chongqing and institutes affiliated to the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Grant No. HZ2021013).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly efficient hydrogen evolution from seawater by a low-cost and stable CoMoP@C electrocatalyst superior to Pt/C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ma Y-Y, Wu C-X, Feng X-J, Tan H-Q, Yan L-K, Liu Y, et al. Highly efficient hydrogen evolution from seawater by a low-cost and stable CoMoP@C electrocatalyst superior to Pt/C. Energy Environ Sci. 2017;10:788-798.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Improved hydrogen evolution reaction performance using MoS2-WS2 heterostructures by physicochemical process

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Vikraman D, Hussain S, Akbar K, Truong L, Kathalingam A, Chun SH, et al. Improved hydrogen evolution reaction performance using MoS2-WS2 heterostructures by physicochemical process. ACS Sustain Chem Eng. 2018;6:8400-8409.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In-situ growth of few-layered MoS2 nanosheets on highly porous carbon aerogel as advanced electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang YF, Zuo LZ, Huang YP, Zhang LS, Lai FL, Fan W, et al. In-situ growth of few-layered MoS2 nanosheets on highly porous carbon aerogel as advanced electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction. ACS Sustain Chem Eng. 2015;3:3140-3148.
